critic_raw:
Preprocessing tokenizers and scalers are fit on the entire dataset before splitting, including test-set patients' feedback text. The vocabulary learned from test-set text informs the embedding layer trained on training data — the embedding matrix covers words introduced only in the test period.

For the test set (months 19-24), patients have no prior feedback in the training or validation windows, making prior adherence rate (rolling 6-month average) unavailable or zero. This feature is listed as a metadata input for both models. In training, patients have prior adherence history; in test, they don't. This creates a distribution shift in a high-signal feature (prior adherence is typically the strongest predictor of future adherence) that systematically disadvantages both models on the test set but particularly affects the BiLSTM, which has more capacity to exploit this feature during training and more opportunity to overfit to its signal.

The adherence outcome window extends 90 days after feedback submission. For feedback submitted in month 12 (the last training month), the 90-day outcome window extends into months 13-15 — the validation period. If these outcomes are extracted from the same EHR record pool, training labels for late-month-12 patients implicitly incorporate events that overlap the validation time window. This is outcome-window leakage at the training/validation boundary.

The comparison is VADER+LR vs. BiLSTM with attention. VADER is a non-trainable fixed lexicon; the BiLSTM learns representations from scratch. The design frames this as an architecture comparison, but the fundamental difference is between a static pretrained lexicon and a task-trained neural representation. This conflates learning paradigm (pretrained vs. from-scratch) with architecture (recurrent vs. rule-based). A fair architecture test would compare VADER+LR against an RNN trained on a pretrained embedding (e.g., GloVe or BioWordVec) so that representation quality is equated.

10,000 records with up to 256 hidden units, up to 300 embedding dimensions, and an attention mechanism over two BiLSTM layers represents a model with ~millions of parameters trained on a small dataset. With a 6:3:1 rough split, the training set has approximately 5,000 records — marginal for this model capacity.
all_issues_raised: Preprocessing tokenizers are fit on the entire dataset including test-set patient text — words appearing only in test-period feedback are included in the training vocabulary and embedding layer.

Prior adherence rate (rolling 6-month average) is zero or unavailable for test-set patients who have no prior feedback history, creating a systematic feature distribution shift in a high-signal predictor between training and test sets.

Adherence outcome windows for feedback submitted in the final training months extend into the validation time window — training labels for late-month-12 patients overlap with the validation period, constituting outcome-window leakage.

The comparison is fixed lexicon VADER versus trained BiLSTM representations — this conflates representation learning paradigm with architecture; a fair test would equate representation quality by giving both models pretrained embeddings.

Training set of ~5,000 records is severely underpowered for a BiLSTM with attention using up to 300-dimensional embeddings and 256 hidden units — the model has capacity for millions of parameters relative to training examples, creating high overfitting risk on the text features.
all_issues_adjudicated: Preprocessing tokenizers are fit on the entire dataset including test-set patient text — words appearing only in test-period feedback are included in the training vocabulary and embedding layer.

Prior adherence rate (rolling 6-month average) is zero or unavailable for test-set patients who have no prior feedback history, creating a systematic feature distribution shift in a high-signal predictor between training and test sets.

Adherence outcome windows for feedback submitted in the final training months extend into the validation time window — training labels for late-month-12 patients overlap with the validation period, constituting outcome-window leakage.

The comparison is fixed lexicon VADER versus trained BiLSTM representations — this conflates representation learning paradigm with architecture; a fair test would equate representation quality by giving both models pretrained embeddings.

Training set of ~5,000 records is severely underpowered for a BiLSTM with attention using up to 300-dimensional embeddings and 256 hidden units — the model has capacity for millions of parameters relative to training examples, creating high overfitting risk on the text features.
